PITTWATER

West-facing bay — the sun peaks on the water at 5:30pm and the surface goes glassy by 4pm. Residents plan their day around this. The timing is not accidental. It is the reason.

SPOTTED GUMS

The canopy creates a microclimate running 3–4°C cooler than open Northern Beaches suburbs on hot days. Now zoning-protected. The tree that made the suburb is the tree the suburb is protecting.

WIND & TIDES

North-easterly breeze arrives by noon — the sailing window opens. Tidal range 1.3m. Residents who sail know the tide table by memory. Those who don’t sail learn it eventually.

Clareville

Northern Beaches

NSW 2107

Guringai Country

Character

Walking through Clareville isn’t like walking through a typical suburb — it’s like wandering through a private coastal forest. No main street. Instead: Hudson Parade, a winding road draped in towering Spotted Gums where architecture ranges from 1950s modernist timber shacks to $20-million concrete masterpieces hidden behind thick foliage. The architecture range is extreme. The foliage that conceals it is consistent. Both are intentional.

The rhythm is dictated by the tide and the sun. Mornings are silent — kookaburras and the gentle lap of water against yacht hulls. Weekends shift to sophisticated leisure: families setting up long lunches at Clareville Beach Reserve, sailors prepping vessels, paddleboarders already back for their second coffee.

 

The Stealth Wealth sanctuary of the Pittwater — a sun-drenched, eucalyptus-scented hideaway where the forest meets the still water, far from the tourist crowds. Top 2% of Sydney suburbs by wealth and price. 12% renting — high owner-occupancy. Average tenure 14 years. The suburb where people arrive and quietly arrange their lives around staying. The arrangement is consistently described as correct.

The 2026 Environmental Overlay zoning protects the Spotted Gum canopy from over-development. The suburb that has found a way to protect the thing that makes it what it is. The canopy remains. The prices rise. Both trends are expected to continue.

38
Distance from CBD
38km
Bus B-Line from Newport, then feeder. Car essential. 2.4 cars per household. Zero traffic lights in the suburb — a distinction maintained by deliberate absence of commercial zoning. Population ~2,100.
22
Pittwater temperature
22°C
Pittwater summer average. Sheltered bay — calm, warm, year-round swimming and paddleboarding. Clareville Beach Reserve faces west. The water goes glassy by 4pm. The reason everyone is home before sunset.
Top
Price percentile
Top 2%
Median house $4.15M (+28% since 2021). Yield 2.1%. Vacancy 1.8%. Median household income $3,450/wk. One of Sydney’s wealthiest suburbs by price and owner-occupancy. The numbers are consistent with the foliage.
48
Median age
48yrs
Population ~2,100. 44% tertiary educated. 12% renting — one of the highest owner-occupancy rates in Sydney. Average tenure 14 years. A suburb of people who found what they were looking for and stopped looking.
The Quiet Cousin of Palm Beach — where celebrities go to disappear, sunsets face west over Pittwater, and the only way to find the best restaurant is to already know about it.

Safety
Who lives here

The Legacy Owners

Families & Weekenders Since the 70s

Held the weekender since it was a shack. Now retired here to sail and garden. The founding community of Clareville — the people who established the precedent that the suburb should remain what it is. Still here. Effective.

The Creative Elite

Architects, Authors & Designers

Drawn to the mid-century aesthetic and the isolation required for deep work. Found in Clareville the particular quiet that produces the particular output they are after. Their studios are in the trees. Their clients are in the city. The distance is considered essential.

The C-Suite Sea-Changer

Remote Executives

Work remotely three to four days a week. Prioritise lifestyle over CBD proximity. Chose Clareville specifically because it required 38km and decided that was the correct distance from the office. Kayak at lunch. Zoom call at 2pm. Sunset at 5:30. This is the schedule.

The Yachties

Passionate Sailors

Choose their home based on proximity to their mooring and the Avalon Sailing Club. Found in Clareville both the mooring and the environment that makes the sailing meaningful. The boat matters. The suburb accommodates this priority without comment.

Life in Clareville

2026
01

Hudson Parade

The winding road through the Spotted Gums that functions as the suburb’s spine. No footpaths. No traffic lights. Architecture ranging from original 1950s shacks to hidden concrete masterpieces. The range is the point — Clareville has never agreed on what a house should look like and has been better for not agreeing.

02

Food & Drink

Every day — coffee at the beach kiosk, Clareville Beach Reserve for the long lunch. The occasion — The Clareville Kiosk: hatted-quality dining in a converted cottage, intimate, no sign on the road, the best restaurant you will eat at this year if someone who knows about it takes you.

03

Clareville Beach Reserve

The suburb’s living room. Families setting up long lunches on weekends. Sailors prepping vessels at the refurbished wharf. Paddleboarders already back from the morning session. The beach that faces west: warm afternoon light, sunset views over the national park, the Pittwater glassy by 4pm.

04

Avalon Sailing Club

Located in Clareville — the hub for youth dinghy racing and weekend yachting. The sailing culture that gives Clareville its social architecture beyond property values. The Wednesday afternoon race. The annual regatta. The particular community that forms around people who all understand which way the wind is going.

05

Pittwater Walkway

Extension completed 2025 — connecting lower Clareville more seamlessly to Avalon. The Taylors Point swimming hole. The coastal wander from Clareville Beach to Paradise Beach — 2km, steep inclines, dramatic views. A walk conducted by people who use “dramatic views” as a baseline expectation and remain correct to do so.

06

2026 Update

The Clareville Beach Wharf restoration complete: improved water taxi access. Environmental Overlay zoning introduced: Spotted Gum canopy protection from McMansion development. Pittwater Walkway extension complete. The suburb protecting, systematically, the things that make it worth protecting.

Resident Voices

01 — Retiree
David, 72
Loves it
I can hear the water from my bed. Forty years of this. The sound has not changed. The price of the house around it has changed considerably. The water is indifferent to both facts.
Frustrates
How hard it is for my grandkids to visit by bus. It is a mission for them. I appreciate the visit more for the effort it requires. I would prefer the bus were easier. Both things are true.
02 — Remote Worker
Sarah, 41
Loves it
The 5G is surprisingly great for Zoom calls. I kayak at lunch. These two sentences describe a working day that I would not trade for anything the CBD is currently offering at equivalent price.
Frustrates
No decent bakery within walking distance. I have adapted. I bake on Sundays. This has improved my bread significantly and not improved my feeling about the situation at all.
03 — Local Teen
Leo, 16
Loves it
Living here is like a movie in summer. The water, the boats, the sunsets. I am aware that this is an unusual sentence for a sixteen-year-old to mean sincerely. I mean it sincerely.
Frustrates
In winter it is a bit lonely if you don’t have your P-plates yet. I don’t have my P-plates yet. Summer returns. I am patient. The water is still there.

The Quiet Cousin of Palm Beach — where the wealthy go to disappear

2026 Update

Protected Enclave — Deliberately

Clareville has spent 2026 protecting itself. The Environmental Overlay zoning. The wharf restoration. The walkway extension. Each decision reinforces the suburb’s status as a protected enclave where the character remains, the prices rise, and the Spotted Gums are not negotiable.

Beach Wharf — Restored
Significant structural restoration complete late 2025 — improved water taxi access for a suburb where the water is the primary transport for anyone without a car. The wharf that connects Clareville to the rest of the Pittwater network.
Environmental Overlay — Introduced
New zoning protecting the Spotted Gum canopy from McMansion over-development — the legislation the community has been requesting. Granted. The trees are protected. The character is protected alongside them.
Pittwater Walkway — Extended
Pedestrian link between Clareville and Avalon improved — more seamless connection to the village without the car. The infrastructure that slightly reduces the isolation that most residents consider the suburb’s primary feature.
